nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rivers and streams with sandy or gravel substrate and moderate to strong current; found in the Mekong, Chao Phraya, Xe Bangfai, and Meklong river basins. Often associated with submerged rocks, root tangles, and macrophyte patches in mid-water.

decorPreferences: multiple caves and rocky overhangs for territory definition
dense planting around tank perimeter (Amazon swords, vallisneria, Java fern)
open sandy or fine-gravel midground for foraging
line-of-sight breaks to reduce inter-specimen aggression if multiple fish are attempted
powerhead or spray bar to simulate moderate current

diet.notes: The Rainbow Shark is a benthic omnivore that grazes on algae films and organic detritus, and accepts a wide range of prepared foods in the aquarium. High-quality sinking pellets, algae wafers, and spirulina-based flakes form a reliable staple, supplemented with blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, or spinach. Frozen and live foods — bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, and tubifex — are taken readily and add variety. In the wild the species feeds on algae, organic detritus, small invertebrates, and plant matter scraped from substrate surfaces. Offering a varied diet with a consistent vegetable component is generally considered beneficial for long-term health and tends to reduce fin-nipping behavior toward tankmates.

goodWith: mid-water schooling fish (large tetras, barbs, danios)
corydoras catfish (with caution — avoid bottom overlap in small tanks)
loaches in large tanks with ample territory
medium-to-large peaceful cichlids in spacious tanks
avoidWith: other Rainbow Sharks (solitary in tanks under 400 L — intense conspecific aggression at maturity)
Red-Tailed Black Shark (Epalzeorhynchos bicolor, fw-fish-124) — closely related, triggers severe territorial conflict
Flying Fox (Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus) — same genus, aggressive interspecific competition
Bala Sharks and other similar-looking Cyprinids in restricted space
small slow-moving or long-finned fish that share the bottom zone
dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates (predation risk)

Wikimedia Commons category for E. frenatum; multiple images from aquarium specimens typically available under CC BY-SA. Confirm specific image license before use.

summary: The Rainbow Shark is a Southeast Asian cyprinid recognized by its dark grey-to-black body and uniformly red-orange fins — a useful distinction from the Red-Tailed Black Shark, which carries red only on the tail. Adults reach 15 cm and become strongly territorial at maturity; most aquariums should house only one.
careNotes:
The Rainbow Shark (Epalzeorhynchos frenatum) is an active bottom-dweller native to the river systems of mainland Southeast Asia, primarily the Mekong, Chao Phraya, Xe Bangfai, and Meklong basins across Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam. Despite the name, it is a true cyprinid — a member of the carp family — and is unrelated to sharks; the name refers to the shark-like body profile and erect dorsal fin. Adults reach 12–15 cm and are recognized by a dark charcoal-grey body with uniformly red-to-orange fins across all fin pairs: dorsal, pectoral, pelvic, anal, and caudal alike. This distinguishes them from the closely related Red-Tailed Black Shark (Epalzeorhynchos bicolor), which carries a fully black body and red only on the caudal fin. A selectively bred albino form — sold in the hobby as the 'Albino Rainbow Shark' — displays a pale white body with red fins and pink eyes and is commonly available alongside the standard form.

A single adult requires a minimum tank of 200 L with a footprint of at least 120 cm. The tank should offer abundant hiding places — caves, overturned terracotta pots, rocky overhangs, and driftwood tangles — since territorial ownership of a defined area tends to reduce stress and aggression toward tankmates. Moderate current from a powerhead or spray bar mimics the species' native riverine conditions. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 5–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5), and maintained between 22 and 27°C.

The Rainbow Shark is widely considered strongly territorial toward conspecifics and closely related genus members, particularly once sexually mature at around 12–18 months. In tanks under approximately 400 L, keeping a single specimen is generally advised. Keepers with very large, heavily decorated tanks of 400 L or more have reported successfully housing groups by providing multiple distinct territories, but this approach is not recommended for beginners. Epalzeorhynchos bicolor (Red-Tailed Black Shark, fw-fish-124) and Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus (Flying Fox) should be avoided as tankmates under any circumstances — both are closely related genus members, and territorial disputes between them and the Rainbow Shark tend to escalate, frequently resulting in injury. Mid-water schooling fish such as barbs, danios, larger tetras, and rainbowfish generally make suitable tankmates as they occupy a different part of the water column. The Rainbow Shark tends to graze actively on algae films and organic detritus and will contribute to substrate cleanliness; it should also receive a varied diet that includes vegetable matter and occasional protein foods.
breedingNotes: The Rainbow Shark is rarely bred in home aquaria. Commercial production in Southeast Asia relies on hormone injection — typically HCG or carp pituitary extract — to induce spawning, which would otherwise be prevented by the intense territorial aggression adults display toward one another. In captivity the species scatters eggs, but pairing two individuals without hormone intervention requires very large, purpose-built facilities, and successful hobbyist spawning accounts are not widely documented. Sexing is difficult; mature females may appear slightly deeper-bodied, but this is not considered a reliable field mark. The Rainbow Shark is generally not regarded as a practical breeding project for the home aquarium.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Epalzeorhynchos frenatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 15 cm), temperature range (22–26°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–15 dH), native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Xe Bangfai, Meklong basins), biotope (river), aggression profile (strongly territorial, solitary recommended), diet (benthic omnivore), and breeding notes (hormone-induced in commercial farms). Tank size recommendation: minimum 200 L for single specimen.

additional.name: FishBase — Epalzeorhynchos frenatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (to 15 cm TL), native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, Thailand/Laos/Cambodia/Vietnam), temperature (22–27°C), and diet classification (omnivore). Confirmed species is not found in Sumatra or Borneo (those records belong to E. kalopterus).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rainbow Shark Care
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Confirmed tank size (55 gal / ~208 L minimum), temperature (72–79°F / 22–26°C), pH 6.5–7.5, semi-aggressive temperament, solitary keeping recommendation, and distinction from Red-Tailed Black Shark. Noted albino variant availability and compatibility considerations with mid-water species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rainbow Shark Profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist reference. Confirmed adult size (up to 15 cm), temperament (territorial, aggressive towards conspecifics and related species), care level (intermediate), diet (omnivore with algae grazing), tank decoration advice (caves, dense planting, line-of-sight breaks), and genus-mate conflict with E. bicolor and E. kalopterus.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Epalzeorhynchos bicolor (for comparative species separation)
additional.notes: Reference entry for the Red-Tailed Black Shark (E. bicolor, fw-fish-124 in DB) used to accurately document morphological distinction: E. bicolor has a fully black body with a red caudal fin only; E. frenatum has a dark grey body with red on all fins. Also confirmed mutual aggression between the two species should they be housed together.

fish.breedingNotes: Commercial production relies on hormone injection — typically HCG or carp pituitary extract — at Southeast Asian fish farms. Hobbyist spawning is not reliably documented; the extreme territorial aggression that develops at maturity makes pairing individuals in a home aquarium impractical.
